narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this isn't easy, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a bit too rocky, with a 52% chance of not passing on the first try. Picture your story through its lens: your Year 3 grades landing you in the 4th decile stood out as the biggest red flag, signaling to the model that your performance dipped when the stakes got higher, pulling hardest toward that failure risk. Layered on top was your Year 1 decile of 6, which didn't show the early spark of top performers who typically power through. Your middle-family income bracket added another layer of concern, as it often correlates with fewer resources for the intense bar prep that many need. Even your LSAT score of 38, while solid, didn't quite counterbalance that in the model's eyes, nudging things further toward struggle. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 echoed a pattern of academic hurdles, and as a white male going full-time, those traits aligned with profiles that historically face steeper odds. The one bright spot was your gender, which slightly tipped toward passing potential, but it couldn't outweigh the combined weight of those other pieces. Together, they painted a picture of someone who's determined—full-time commitment and all—but whose track record suggests the bar might demand more targeted boosts to conquer. This isn't a judgment on your worth or drive; it's just the model highlighting where others with stronger grade momentum have sailed through. You've got time to rethink, maybe retake that LSAT or build those deciles higher—law's a marathon, and pivots like yours have turned into triumphs before. Hang in there; this is one chapter, not the whole book.
